text: The video shows a puckered area of tissue within the sigmoid colon, indicative of the cancerous region, as it moves back and forth.
A five French open-ended catheter is inserted into the right ureter, with IC green dye being injected to enhance visualization of the ureter's path.
The surgeon lateralizes the sigmoid colon, demonstrating the technique's effectiveness in repositioning the colon. The video is sped up for presentation purposes.
The surgeon uses infrared light to visualize the ureter, which appears as a green structure, enhancing its identification during the procedure.

The surgeon ensures a margin of at least five centimeters distal and proximal from the cancerous tissue, excising a generous portion of mesentery extending to the inferior mesenteric artery to obtain sufficient lymph nodes for staging.
The surgeon exposes the inferior mesenteric artery, completing the dissection of the mesentery. Due to the artery's size and inability to be desiccated properly, it is clipped.
The surgeon has completely removed the specimen, revealing a redundant section of the colon suitable for a side-to-side anastomosis. The splenic flexure was mobilized earlier in the procedure.

The surgeon notes the lesion's position approximately twenty-six centimeters from the anal verge, highlighting the mobilization and redundancy of the colon that facilitates achieving an adequate margin. The surgeon performs a hand-sewn intracorporeal anastomosis.
The surgeon is working on the posterior serosal layer of a two-layered anastomosis, using interrupted sutures to secure the back row of the anastomosis.

The surgeon begins suturing the mucosal layer, the innermost layer of the anastomosis, using a running stitch technique. Vicryl sutures are used for this layer, and canal stitches are applied at the ends and edges.
The surgeon uses a 3-0 Vicryl suture on an SH needle to perform a hand-sewn repair, employing robotic assistance to replicate the technique used in open or laparoscopic procedures.
